companyCaseStudy.title: How GitLab Scaled Git Operations: Gitaly, Praefect, and shaving 4x off RPC latency
companyCaseStudy.summary: When Git RPCs started to drown GitLab.com, engineers extracted Git I/O out of the monolith into a purpose-built service (Gitaly), added a replication/proxy layer (Praefect), and reworked storage and caching — cutting median Git RPC latency by ~4x, reducing app-server CPU by ~60%, and enabling an order-of-magnitude increase in repo throughput.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook
GitLab.com faced a growing, painful bottleneck: repository traffic (pushes, clones, fetches) was exploding and the monolith that proxied Git work was becoming the throttle. At peak, Git RPCs were spiking into the tens of thousands per minute and certain repo operations showed median latencies of ~200ms+ — affecting CI, web UX, and developer productivity.

This is the story of how GitLab moved Git handling into Gitaly, added Praefect for replication and routing, and rebuilt the storage/caching layer so Git operations could scale without dragging the entire platform down.

---

## Problem: Git workload is different — and it was killing the monolith

Git RPCs are I/O-heavy, bursty, and latency-sensitive. On GitLab.com the monolith handled web requests, CI orchestration, and Git RPC proxying. That created these pain points:

- Spiky CPU and I/O on app servers during mass clones/fetches (release windows, CI peaks) — a few repos would cause system-wide slowdown.
- High median Git RPC latencies (200ms+ for many operations) that compounded into slower CI and slow web repo pages.
- Hard-to-scale shared storage and contention when many clients requested packfiles or loose objects.
- Operational complexity when trying to replicate repositories between data centers or zones.

### Constraints & requirements

- Keep existing Git protocol compatibility (SSH/HTTP) for users.
- Make repo operations horizontally scalable and isolate failures from the monolith.
- Support replication for HA and geo-distribution without introducing per-request complexity to the monolith.

---

## Solution: split, specialize, replicate

GitLab's engineers designed a three-part approach:

1. Gitaly: a dedicated Git RPC server that runs Git operations close to repository storage.
2. Praefect: a proxy/router that understands repository placement and handles replication and fail-over logic.
3. Storage & caching improvements: local packfile caches, object caching, and smarter placement/sharding of repositories.

This allowed Git RPCs to be handled by purpose-built services (Gitaly servers) rather than the monolith.

### Architecture (high level)

```mermaid
graph LR
  User[Developer client (git clone/push/fetch)] --> LB[Load Balancer]
  LB --> Router[Praefect (proxy & routing)]
  Router --> G1[Gitaly - Storage Node A]
  Router --> G2[Gitaly - Storage Node B]
  G1 --> StorageA[Repository Disk / Object Cache]
  G2 --> StorageB[Repository Disk / Object Cache]
  G1 --> DB[(Postgres: repo metadata)]
  G2 --> DB
  Metrics --> Prom[Prometheus/Grafana]
  subgraph Monolith
    Web[GitLab Rails app]
  end
  Web --> Router
  Web --> DB
```

### Key design decisions

- Separate responsibilities: the Rails monolith handles web/UI/auth; Gitaly handles Git primitives (receive-pack, upload-pack, etc.). That isolates CPU and I/O.
- Use gRPC between Rails and Gitaly for efficient streaming of packfiles and to avoid HTTP-layer overhead for large data transfer.
- Push caching and packfile reuse: co-locate pack caches on Gitaly nodes so repeated downloads (clones/fetches) are satisfied from warm caches.
- Pragmatic replication: Praefect routes a request to the primary replica and fans writes to secondaries; on read-heavy traffic Praefect can route to nearest replica.
- Observability-first: Prometheus metrics from Gitaly + Grafana dashboards for RPC latency, packfile cache hit rate, disk throughput, and gRPC stream errors.

---

## Implementation details & trade-offs

- Language: Gitaly was implemented as a standalone service (Go + gRPC) to be efficient at streaming and lightweight on memory/CPU.
- Protocols: Existing user-facing protocols (SSH, HTTPS) remain unchanged — the monolith still authenticates and hands long-running Git ops to Gitaly.
- Storage: repositories stored on network-attached storage for flexibility, but hot caches (packfile caches) are kept local on Gitaly nodes to reduce repeated NFS/object-store reads.
- Replication strategy: asynchronous file-replication with consistency checks; Praefect acts as the authoritative router and can detect unhealthy replicas and failover.

Trade-offs:
- Added operational surface (Gitaly + Praefect) but gained predictable scaling for repo traffic.
- Some complexity in ensuring strong-enough consistency for writes across replicas — Git semantics allow eventual consistency in many workflows, but writes require careful primary routing.

---

## Results: concrete improvements

| Metric | Before | After |
|--------|--------|-------|
| Median Git RPC latency (upload-pack, receive-pack) | ~200ms | ~50ms |
| App-server CPU during repo spikes | 80% | 30% |
| Throughput for concurrent repo operations | ~1,000 ops/min | ~10,000 ops/min |
| Packfile cache hit rate | 10–20% | 70–85% |

Operational outcomes:

- The monolith was no longer the choke point: repo blasts (mass clones during releases) no longer caused site-wide slowdowns.
- Gitaly nodes could be scaled horizontally to absorb traffic; adding nodes increased throughput linearly until a new storage bottleneck appeared.
- Praefect made failover and replica management operationally simple: during a storage node failure, routing and recovery were automated.

---

## Visualizing the flow (simple flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[git client] --> B[LB]
  B --> C[Praefect (choose primary replica)]
  C --> D[Gitaly primary (apply write)]
  D --> E[Replicate to secondaries]
  E --> F[Confirm to client]
```

---

## Lessons learned

- Don’t treat Git traffic the same as web traffic: moving heavy, streaming workloads into a purpose-built service reduces cross-contamination.
- Caching matters: caches for packfiles and objects give huge wins because many clones/fetches reuse the same data.
- Observability drives safety: fine-grained metrics on stream durations, cache hits, and replica lag made the system operable.
- Design for graceful degradation: read routing to replicas plus quick failover means user-visible disruptions are minimized.

## What changed for engineers day-to-day

- Faster pushes and clones = faster CI and developer feedback loops.
- Simplified scaling: add Gitaly nodes or scale storage independently from Rails.
- Clearer incident boundaries: Git problems show up in Gitaly dashboards instead of taking down the whole site.

---

## Why this story matters

Git operations are a distinct class of traffic. By extracting and optimizing for that workload (streaming RPCs, large binary transfers, and cacheable packfiles), GitLab turned a chronic operational problem into a horizontally scalable service, improving both user experience and platform reliability.
keyTakeaways: Specialize for heavy, streaming workloads — move them out of the monolith. GitLab’s Gitaly cut median Git RPC latency from ~200ms to ~50ms by co-locating Git I/O and using gRPC.
Cache aggressively and locally: packfile/object caches can raise hit rates from ~10–20% to 70–85%, dramatically reducing network I/O and latency.
Use a lightweight routing layer (Praefect) for replication and failover — it simplifies HA and allows independent scaling of compute and storage.

concept.title: Sagas: How to Do Distributed 'Transactions' Without Holding the World Hostage
concept.summary: Sagas let you coordinate multi-step, cross-service business operations (like booking travel or transferring money) without blocking distributed systems with 2PC. They trade *immediate atomicity* for *eventual consistency* using local transactions plus compensating actions.
concept.explanation:
## WHY it matters — a real-world problem

Imagine a travel site that books flight, hotel, and car rental across three different services. If the flight succeeds, the hotel fails, and the car succeeds, what should the system do? Traditional ACID transactions across services (2PC) are slow, brittle, and don't fit microservices or cloud-first architectures. *You still need a correct end state.*

Enter the **Saga Pattern** — a way to model a multi-step business operation as a sequence of local transactions, each with a compensating action to undo it if a later step fails.

### Analogy — Dominoes with Undo Buttons

Think of a saga like laying dominoes (each local transaction). If one domino knocks over the next (success), great. If one domino breaks, instead of trying to rebuild them all atomically, you trigger *undo actions* for the already-fallen dominoes in reverse order. The system ends in a consistent state, even if it required some compensations.

---

## Two flavors: Orchestration vs Choreography

- **Orchestration**: A central saga orchestrator tells services what to do and handles compensations when something fails. Good for complex flows and visibility.
- **Choreography**: Services publish events and other services react to those events; compensations are triggered by event-driven reactions. Good for simple flows and loose coupling.

### Sequence Diagram (Orchestration)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  participant Client
  participant Orchestrator
  participant Flight
  participant Hotel
  participant Car

  Client->>Orchestrator: StartBooking{user, itinerary}
  Orchestrator->>Flight: BookFlight
  Flight-->>Orchestrator: FlightBooked
  Orchestrator->>Hotel: BookHotel
  Hotel-->>Orchestrator: HotelFailed
  Orchestrator->>Flight: CancelFlight (compensate)
  Flight-->>Orchestrator: FlightCanceled
  Orchestrator-->>Client: BookingFailed
```

### Flowchart (Choreography example)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[User places order] --> B[Service A processes]
  B -->|success| C[emit A.succeeded event]
  C --> D[Service B handles event]
  D -->|fails| E[emit B.failed event]
  E --> F[Service A runs compensate]
```

---

## BEFORE / AFTER: Two approaches

| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|----------|------|------|-------------|
| **Monolithic / Distributed 2PC** | Strong ACID guarantees, simple mental model | Blocks resources, poor availability, hard across microservices | Single DB or tightly-coupled systems where latency/availability is OK |
| **Saga (orchestrated / choreographed)** | High availability, fits microservices, non-blocking | Eventual consistency, needs compensating actions & complexity | Long-running business processes across services |

---

## Practical example (orchestrator) — high level

Steps for a hotel/flight booking saga:
1. Book flight (local transaction on Flight service)
2. Book hotel (local transaction on Hotel service)
3. Charge payment (local transaction on Payments service)

If step 2 fails, run the compensating transaction for step 1 (cancel flight). If step 3 fails, compensate 2 and 1.

### Key properties to design into each step

- **Each step is a local ACID transaction** within its service boundary.
- **Each step has a compensating action** (e.g., cancel reservation, refund).
- **Saga state is durable** — store current step, status, and retry attempts.
- **Operations are idempotent** so retries don't cause duplicate effects.

### Simple orchestrator pseudocode (conceptual)

```js
// conceptual Node.js saga orchestrator
const steps = [bookFlight, bookHotel, chargePayment];
const compensations = [cancelFlight, cancelHotel, refundPayment];

async function runSaga(ctx) {
  const completed = [];
  try {
    for (let i=0;i<steps.length;i++) {
      await steps[i](ctx);
      completed.push(i);
    }
    return { status: 'COMPLETED' };
  } catch (err) {
    // compensate in reverse order
    for (let i = completed.length-1; i>=0; i--) {
      try { await compensations[i](ctx); } catch (cErr) { /* log & retry */ }
    }
    return { status: 'FAILED', reason: err.message };
  }
}
```

### Choreography snippet (event-driven)

- Service A publishes `OrderPlaced`.
- Service B listens and publishes `InventoryReserved` or `InventoryFailed`.
- Service C listens and publishes `PaymentCaptured` or `PaymentFailed`.
- If `PaymentFailed`, services that previously published success events listen for this and run compensations.

This avoids a single orchestrator but increases the difficulty of reasoning about the whole flow.

---

## Common pitfalls and how to avoid them

- **No compensating action**: If an operation can't be undone (e.g., external irreversible side-effect), design a compensating *mitigation* (refund, credit, manual process).
- **Non-idempotent operations**: Retrying without idempotency leads to duplicates. Use unique operation IDs and safe retry semantics.
- **Long-running sagas without timeouts**: They can leave resources reserved. Use TTLs and explicit timeouts.
- **Lost saga state**: Persist saga state (status, responses, retries) in durable storage so orchestrators can recover and continue.
- **Observability gaps**: Without clear logs/traces you won't know why compensations ran. Correlate saga IDs in logs and traces.
- **Ordering dependencies**: Ensure order of operations and compensations is clearly defined; race conditions between services can lead to inconsistent compensations.

---

## When to use Sagas (quick checklist)

- You need a business-level transaction that spans multiple services.
- Strong immediate consistency is *not* required; eventual consistency is acceptable.
- You can define compensating actions or mitigations for every critical step.

If you need strict ACID across services and cannot tolerate eventual consistency, Sagas are not a fit (but you should also question your cross-service transaction boundaries).

---

## Operational tips

- Make every step and compensation **idempotent**.
- Persist saga state in a durable store (DB, state store, or workflow engine like Temporal/Step Functions).
- Use exponential backoff and limited retries for transient failures.
- Add observability: saga-id correlation, step timestamps, and reason codes for compensations.
- Prefer orchestration for complex flows and choreography for simple, highly decoupled pipelines.

---

## Final analogy recap

Sagas are like a well-prepared dinner party: you cook appetizers, the main, and dessert in sequence. If the oven breaks while making the main, you don't try to rewind time — you pivot: cancel the main, serve more appetizers and offer a coupon (compensation). The party continues, maybe not exactly as planned, but everyone leaves with a fair outcome.
keyTakeaways: Sagas break a cross-service business operation into local transactions plus compensations to achieve eventual consistency.
Choose orchestration (central controller) for visibility/complex flows or choreography (events) for looser coupling.
Design compensations, make operations idempotent, persist saga state, and add observability and retries.
